questions.question: When the writer mentions elephants and buffalo, she is making the point that
options: they could possibly be a threat to Nhamo.
Nhamo does not believe stories she has heard about them.
the villagers are unaware of the danger in the forest.
a girl on her own is more likely to be attacked by them.
questions.correct_answer: they could possibly be a threat to Nhamo.
questions.explanation: The text uses 'although' clauses to concede both animals are present: elephants 'migrated to the river at certain times of the year' and 'someone had' run into a buffalo 'a few months earlier'. The phrasing 'She didn't expect to... She wasn't likely to...' downplays but does not dismiss the danger — the writer is acknowledging they could possibly be a threat, even if unlikely. Option B is wrong because Nhamo doesn't disbelieve the stories. Option C is wrong because the villagers clearly know about the dangers. Option D is unsupported — the text doesn't compare danger for girls versus others.

questions.question: When she crosses the stream, Nhamo is most worried by
questions.correct_answer: the deep water.
questions.explanation: The key word is 'most worried'. During the crossing itself, the text says 'She always experienced a moment of panic in the middle because she couldn't swim' and 'The water came up to her shoulders.' Her panic comes from the depth of the water combined with her inability to swim. Crocodiles are checked for before she enters (she picks a spot with clear yellow sand). Leeches are dealt with after she crosses — an annoyance, not a worry during crossing. The river bed is described as 'coarse sand', not uneven.

questions.question: One advantage of the deserted village for Nhamo is that
options: it is near to the river.
no one else knows about it.
she can collect wood without any trouble.
questions.correct_answer: she can collect wood without any trouble.
questions.explanation: The text states directly: 'That was the good thing about this place: the wood was easy to find. It took her far less time than Aunt Chipo suspected.' This is the explicitly named advantage. Option A is not stated — she crosses the stream to reach it, but 'near' is not mentioned. Option B is partly true (her people don't visit), but the text says 'some said the place was haunted' — people know about it. Option C: vegetables have 'gone wild', but she doesn't gather them; her purpose is firewood and her secret treasures.

questions.question: The word 'reverently' tells us
options: that Nhamo believes the possessions have supernatural powers.
how precious the articles hidden in the hole are to Nhamo.
that Nhamo is careful in the way she handles the possessions of others.
how valuable some everyday articles are in Nhamo's village.
questions.correct_answer: how precious the articles hidden in the hole are to Nhamo.
questions.explanation: 'Reverently' means with deep respect or devotion. The items are called 'treasures' and are hidden in a secret hole — they are precious to Nhamo personally. She smooths out the cloth and arranges the utensils with care because they matter deeply to her. Option A is wrong — there's no suggestion of supernatural belief. Option C is misleading — some items originally belonged to others (Aunt Chipo's cloth, Uncle Kufa's knife), but she treats them reverently because they are precious to her, not out of respect for their original owners. Option D broadens the point too much — the question is about what 'reverently' tells us about Nhamo specifically.

questions.question: When the writer tells us about how magazines were used in Nhamo's village, we learn that
options: most people are only interested in the pictures.
they are a window to the world outside the village.
most people laugh at the stories they contain.
they are more valued as fuel than as reading matter.
questions.correct_answer: they are a window to the world outside the village.
questions.explanation: The passage describes multiple uses: men read and retell stories 'to everyone's enjoyment', women study pictures of 'clothes and houses, gardens and cars' and copy hairstyles. The magazines show the villagers things from the outside world they would never otherwise see. Option A is wrong — the men retell the stories and everyone enjoys them, so it's not only the pictures. Option C is wrong — they enjoy the stories, they don't laugh at them. Option D is wrong — the magazines are eventually used as fire-starters only after they 'fell apart', not because they were more valued as fuel. The overall picture is that magazines bring glimpses of the wider world.

questions.question: The main reason why Nhamo is attracted to the magazine photograph is that
options: it is the only picture she has of her mother.
it includes a child who looks just like her.
questions.correct_answer: it shows a loving relationship.
questions.explanation: The text says 'what interested Nhamo most was the little girl' and 'The woman smiled at her in the kindest way, and Nhamo knew the white bread and yellow margarine were meant for the little girl.' Nhamo then thinks 'the woman looked like Mother' and uses the picture to play-act conversations with 'Mother'. The core attraction is the warm, loving mother-daughter relationship depicted — which Nhamo lost at age three. Option A is wrong — it's not actually her mother, she just imagines it is. Option B is unsupported — the text doesn't say the child looks like Nhamo. Option D: while the room has 'wonderful things', the text explicitly says what interested her 'most' was the relationship between the woman and child.

questions.question: The title of the poem
options: hints that what follows will be from the cat's perspective.
encourages the reader to admire the cat.
is reflected in the senses that are described.
prepares the reader for a surprising event.
questions.correct_answer: is reflected in the senses that are described.
questions.explanation: The title is 'Observing the Mute Cat'. 'Observing' relates to the sense of sight, and 'Mute' relates to the sense of hearing (or its absence). The poem then describes the cat through sight (watching him run, climb, hunt), sound (he 'mimes a greeting mew', 'rattling' the flyscreen, 'eloquent of purr'), and touch ('walk on his man'). The title's two key words — observing and mute — are directly reflected in the senses explored throughout. Option A is wrong — it's from the human's perspective observing the cat. Option B is too narrow. Option D is wrong — there is no single surprising event.

questions.question: What do we learn about the person in the poem?
options: He prefers to spend time with the cat outdoors.
He respects the wishes of the cat.
He forgets to leave the cat enough food.
He is impressed by what the cat catches.
questions.correct_answer: He respects the wishes of the cat.
questions.explanation: The poem directly states: 'he politely hates to be picked up. His human friend never does it.' The human knows the cat dislikes being picked up and respects that wish by never doing it. Option A is unsupported — indoor and outdoor preference isn't discussed. Option C is wrong — the cat has a food dispenser and 'preferring to be served' implies the human does serve him. Option D is wrong — the birds are mentioned as being carried off 'discreetly', suggesting the human barely notices rather than being impressed.

questions.question: The cat makes a sound using
questions.correct_answer: the flyscreen.
questions.explanation: The poem says: 'He finds a voice / in the flyscreen, rattling it'. The cat is described as mute — he 'mimes a greeting mew' (no sound). But he 'finds a voice' by rattling the flyscreen to communicate that he wants to be let in. The tail is described as 'indignant' but doesn't make a sound. The pear tree and dispenser are not associated with sound-making.

questions.question: What does the cat take away?
questions.correct_answer: birds
questions.explanation: The final stanza says: 'discreet with his few stained birds / he carries them off to read.' The cat carries away birds. The mouse 'ran in under him' and escaped — the stanza describes him losing the mouse, then pouncing. Pellets are in the dispenser but he waits to be served. 'A fish' is not mentioned as something he takes away — 'fish' appears only as a verb ('He can fish food pellets out').

questions.question: The cat acts independently when he
questions.correct_answer: accesses drinking water.
questions.explanation: The opening stanza shows the cat making his own choice: 'Clean water in the house / but the cat laps up clay water / outside. Drinking the earth.' He independently chooses to drink muddy water outside rather than the clean water provided indoors. Option A is partially true (he waits to be served rather than using the dispenser) but that's more about being demanding than independent. Option B: he needs the flyscreen rattled to be let in — not independent. Option D: 'His pile, being perfect, ignores the misting rain' — he stays put in rain, but this describes his fur rather than an active independent choice.

questions.question: Mikey is aware that the things thrown at him
options: are landing near him by mistake.
are meant to make him retaliate.
are coming from two different places.
are not intended to hurt him.
questions.correct_answer: are not intended to hurt him.
questions.explanation: The text says 'At least it was just soft stuff coming his way' — a tree root and a clump of mud. The phrase 'at least it was just soft stuff' shows Mikey recognises the projectiles are not meant to cause real harm. Option A is wrong — he knows it's deliberate ('it confirmed that he was under some sort of attack'). Option B has no support. Option C is wrong — both came from the same spot ('behind a large mound').

questions.question: After the meeting at the bridge, Zak and Tremble are thinking that Mikey
options: is hiding the fact that he belongs to another group.
has witnessed an incident where a bird got injured.
deserves their respect for showing courage.
may be a legendary figure sent to fight The Strider.
questions.correct_answer: deserves their respect for showing courage.
questions.explanation: Celery (Tremble) says 'he's an absolute legend!' after recognising Mikey as the one who rescued the sparrowhawk from near the Strider. Zak adds 'You're all right, you are — You just stand and don't run.' They admire his bravery in rescuing the bird and in standing his ground rather than fleeing. Option B is inaccurate — Mikey didn't merely witness an injury, he actively rescued the bird. Option D takes 'legend' too literally. Option A is unsupported.

questions.question: In the paragraph about The Strider, we are told that there is some disagreement over
options: how best to fight against him.
questions.correct_answer: what his exact form is.
questions.explanation: The text says: 'Some said he was a malevolent water spirit, some that he was the ghost of a walker who'd tried and failed to jump over the river.' This is a disagreement about what form The Strider takes — spirit vs ghost. By contrast, 'all agreed that he lived in the murky, fast-flowing, waters' (no disagreement on location) and 'all agreed... he liked nothing better than luring people to their watery doom' (no disagreement on intentions). Option D is never mentioned.

questions.question: What does Emily do at The Crossing?
options: She boasts about surviving the fall.
She tries to provoke The Strider into appearing.
questions.correct_answer: She tries to reassure Mikey.
questions.explanation: Emily says: 'Don't listen to Zak – he's talking mumbo jumbo. It's perfectly easy, Mikey. I'll show you what to do first. I've done it thousands of times. There's a knack to it.' She is directly reassuring Mikey that the task is safe and manageable. Option A is too strong — she dismisses Zak's exaggeration but doesn't tell him off. Option C is wrong — she hasn't fallen. Option D is unsupported.

questions.question: How does Mikey feel about crossing the Stride river?
options: concerned about the condition of the rope
annoyed that this is the only way of joining the club
confident that he can do it the hard way
frustrated that the required method is too complicated
questions.correct_answer: confident that he can do it the hard way
questions.explanation: Mikey estimates the river at 'about eight feet across', notes 'the bank looked sturdy', and thinks 'eight feet wasn't that far: he'd jumped much further in long jump practice.' He says 'Ok, I'll do it!' but then adds 'But not the swing — I'll jump the Stride.' He rejects the easy method (the swing) and confidently chooses to jump — the harder way. Options A, B, and D describe negative emotions that aren't reflected in the text.

questions.question: In this text, the writer uses
options: humour to make the story seem less menacing.
poetic language to make the place seem attractive.
repetition of particular words to make the river seem mysterious.
changes in perspective so that we see the story through different people's eyes.
questions.correct_answer: humour to make the story seem less menacing.
questions.explanation: The story involves potentially threatening situations (being pelted, The Strider legend, a dangerous river crossing), but the writer lightens the tone with humour throughout: Tremble's name (he shakes at the mention of The Strider), describing someone as 'just like a stick of celery', Rango's enormous cowboy hat, 'mumbo jumbo', 'something cowboy-ish from somewhere beneath his hat', 'He's gonna fly like a Sparrow!' These comic touches make the menacing elements feel less threatening. Option B is wrong — the language isn't notably poetic. Option C — while 'Strider/Stride' recur, the main technique is humour. Option D is wrong — the story stays in Mikey's perspective throughout.

questions.question: In the first four lines, the poet repeats words and ideas to suggest
options: how careful he is in his observations.
how quickly he carries out his observations.
how difficult it is to choose a place for his observations.
questions.correct_answer: how careful he is in his observations.
questions.explanation: The first four lines repeat 'very small' twice and pair 'concentrating' with 'focusing my attention' — two words for the same careful, deliberate act. The repetition emphasises precision and care, not speed or difficulty. Option A is about significance, which isn't the focus of these lines. Option C is contradicted by the slow, deliberate tone. Option D is unsupported — the poet has already chosen his spot.

questions.question: In line 9, what becomes a forest?
questions.correct_answer: a crack
questions.explanation: The sentence structure is: 'focusing my attention on a very small area / like this crack in sandstone / perpetually wet with seepage, / getting so close / to moss, liverworts, and fern / it becomes a forest'. The 'it' refers back to the subject being observed — 'this crack in sandstone'. The crack, with its tiny mosses and ferns, is what transforms into a forest when viewed closely enough. The sandstone is just the material the crack is in; seepage is moisture; fern is part of what grows there.

questions.question: The poet uses rhyming words
options: to provide a climax to the poem.
to suggest things are happening now.
to connect the beginning and end of the poem.
questions.correct_answer: to connect the beginning and end of the poem.
questions.explanation: The main rhyming pair is 'introspection' (line 19) and 'direction' (line 21), which occur at the poem's climax. The poem builds gradually through description, and these rhyming words deliver the turning point: others might think he's 'lost in introspection', but 'No! I am awake, absorbed, just looking in a different direction.' The rhyme underscores this emphatic conclusion. Additionally, 'concentrating' and 'attention' at the start echo the -tion sound, linking the beginning to the end. Option D captures this structural connection across the poem.

questions.question: In lines 14 to 19, the poet is
options: complaining about the way people criticise him.
explaining how to understand the complexity of nature.
giving an account of how he feels when observing things.
describing the impression another person may have of him.
questions.correct_answer: describing the impression another person may have of him.
questions.explanation: Lines 14-19: 'Someone seeing me / staring so fixedly / at nothing / might be excused / for thinking me vague, abstracted, / lost in introspection.' The poet imagines how an outside observer would perceive him — staring at nothing, appearing vague and lost in thought. He's describing the impression he'd give. Option A is wrong — 'might be excused' shows understanding, not complaint. Option B is not what these lines address. Option C is wrong — he's describing how others see him, not how he himself feels.

questions.question: In what 'different direction' (line 21) is the poet looking?
questions.correct_answer: into his imagination
questions.explanation: The poem describes looking at a tiny crack in sandstone so closely that it 'becomes a forest with wild beasts in it, birds in the branches and crickets piping'. The real crack is transformed through the poet's imagination into a forest landscape. The 'different direction' is inward — into his imagination, where the miniature world becomes something vast and alive. Option A is partially true (he looks at a real crack), but the 'different direction' is the imaginative transformation, not the physical scene. Options C and D are unsupported.

questions.question: How are the children feeling at the beginning of the extract?
options: depressed by the shabbiness of the room
frustrated by the rules they have to follow
questions.correct_answer: unsure how to occupy themselves
questions.explanation: The children 'sat sadly round the fire' after the baby was put to bed. Jane wishes magic were taught at school so 'it might make something happen.' Robert looks around the room for ideas but gets none. Cyril yawns and says 'We may as well play magic.' They are bored and casting about for something to do. Option A is wrong — the baby is mentioned only as context for timing. Option B — while the room is described as faded/drab, they aren't depressed by it; Robert is just looking for inspiration. Option C has no support in the text.

questions.question: The children try to make magic by
options: using everyday objects they have at home.
reciting a sequence of words from a book.
remembering instructions they were taught in the past.
questions.correct_answer: using everyday objects they have at home.
questions.explanation: The children use chalk (to trace figures), cedar-wood pencil ends (burned for smell), eucalyptus oil ('we have for colds'), and tea-cloths from the kitchen. All are ordinary household items repurposed for their magic attempt. Option A is wrong — they aren't adapting traditions. Option C — they reference the Ingoldsby Legends book for abracadabra but the text doesn't say they recite words from it; instead they draw chalk figures and burn things. Option D is unsupported — nobody taught them magic.

questions.question: When Robert tries to move the egg from under the fireplace grate,
options: he sees it has something inside it.
he is unable to keep hold of it.
he burns his hand on the grate.
questions.correct_answer: he is unable to keep hold of it.
questions.explanation: The text says: 'he put his hand under the grating and picked up the egg. But the egg was much hotter than anyone would have believed... and he dropped it.' He picked it up but couldn't keep hold of it because of the heat. Option A is wrong — he doesn't see anything inside at this point (that comes later after it falls into the fire). Option B is wrong — the egg doesn't crack until it's in the fire. Option D is wrong — he burns himself on the egg, not the grate.

questions.question: What detail particularly annoys the Phoenix about the encyclopaedia entry?
options: the fact that its tail is ignored
the mention of its great age
questions.correct_answer: the reference to a worm
questions.explanation: The Phoenix calls the worm account 'just a vulgar insult' — the strongest language it uses about any part of the entry. It is annoyed by other inaccuracies (body colour, tail colour) but responds to those with mild corrections. The word 'vulgar insult' signals particular annoyance. Option B — the tail isn't ignored; it's described as white, which the Phoenix corrects but without the same intensity. Option C — it dismisses the Phoenicians ('Never mind what they gave') but isn't particularly annoyed. Option D — it agrees antiquity is correct.

questions.question: The language used by the Phoenix
options: is copied from that of the children.
is a mixture of formal and less formal styles.
is difficult for the children to follow.
questions.correct_answer: is a mixture of formal and less formal styles.
questions.explanation: The Phoenix uses formal phrases like 'I am your grateful debtor', 'with a high-bred air', 'in ornithology, fabulous bird of antiquity' — but also informal expressions like 'well, I simply ask you, is it white?', 'just a vulgar insult', 'I can't tell you how weary I got of it.' This mix of elevated and conversational register is characteristic throughout. Option A is wrong — its language is varied. Option B is wrong — its style is distinctly different from the children's. Option D is unsupported — the children understand it fine.

questions.question: What impression is given of the Phoenix throughout this extract?
options: It has a high opinion of itself.
It could be dangerous if angered.
It has been mistreated in the past.
It is unsure of this new environment.
questions.correct_answer: It has a high opinion of itself.
questions.explanation: The Phoenix is 'quite pleased' that its fame has lasted 2000 years, dismisses the encyclopaedia portrait as 'not a flattering likeness', demands corrections to inaccurate descriptions of its appearance, calls the worm story a 'vulgar insult', and speaks with 'a high-bred air.' Throughout, it displays vanity and self-importance. Option B — there's no sense of danger. Option C — 'weary' of its existence but not mistreated. Option D — it seems perfectly comfortable and self-assured.

questions.question: In verse 1, what is the ocean compared with?
questions.correct_answer: birds
questions.explanation: The simile is: 'And like the wings of sea-birds / Flash the white caps of the sea.' The white caps (foam crests) of the ocean are compared to the wings of sea-birds — both white, flashing. The ocean's whitecaps are likened to birds. Option A — clouds aren't part of the comparison. Option C — 'caps' are the ocean feature being described, not what it's compared to. Option D — wind is mentioned separately.

questions.question: In verse 5, the writer
options: wonders what the child is thinking about the storm.
questions whether the child is safe from the storm.
remembers his own experiences of storms as a child.
suggests that the child need not be afraid of the storm.
questions.correct_answer: wonders what the child is thinking about the storm.
questions.explanation: Verse 5 asks: 'What tale do the roaring ocean, / And the night-wind, bleak and wild, / As they beat at the crazy casement, / Tell to that little child?' The poet wonders what story/impression the storm communicates to the child — what the child thinks or imagines. Option B is wrong — safety isn't questioned. Option C is unsupported — no personal memory is invoked. Option D is wrong — the tone is questioning, not reassuring.

questions.question: The poem suggests that the woman is
options: irritated by the noise of the storm.
fearful for the return of the fisherman.
questions.correct_answer: fearful for the return of the fisherman.
questions.explanation: The poem is set in a 'fisherman's cottage' during a violent storm. The woman (mother) paces 'to and fro' — a classic sign of anxiety. Verse 6 asks why the ocean and wind 'beat at the heart of the mother' and 'Drive the color from her cheek' — she is pale with fear. Her husband the fisherman is presumably out at sea in this storm. Option A is unsupported. Option B — she's not irritated, she's frightened. Option C — 'respectful' is too mild for someone going pale.

questions.question: Which of the following does the writer describe as having a human emotion?
questions.correct_answer: the twilight (verse 1)
questions.explanation: Verse 1 opens: 'The twilight is sad and cloudy.' Sadness is a human emotion attributed to the twilight — this is personification. Option A — the cottage 'shines' but isn't given emotion. Option B — the shadow 'passes to and fro' and 'bows' but these are physical movements, not emotions. Option D — the ocean 'roars' and 'beats' — actions, not emotions.

questions.question: The scene in this poem is viewed from
options: between the sea and the cottage.
above the sea, as a bird's-eye view.
questions.correct_answer: between the sea and the cottage.
questions.explanation: The observer sees the outside (the sea, whitecaps, wind) and also sees 'a little face at the window' and 'a woman's waving shadow' — these are details visible from outside the cottage, looking in through the window. The viewpoint is somewhere between the sea and the cottage, seeing both. Option A is wrong — you couldn't see a face at the window from far out at sea. Option B is wrong — if inside, you wouldn't describe the face 'peering out into the night' or see a shadow on the window from inside. Option D is unsupported.
